# Poisson regression with a log link

Counts — fish per transect, eggs per nest, individuals per quadrat — are not
Gaussian. They are non-negative integers, and their variance grows with their
mean. The **Poisson** distribution is the natural starting point.

## The model

We cannot model the rate $\lambda$ directly with a line, because a line can go
negative. Instead we model it on the **log scale** — the **log link**:

$$
\text{count}_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad
\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta\,x_i
$$

In FANGS:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    count[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta * x[i]
  }
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta  ~ dnorm(0, 10)
}
```

## Interpreting on the log scale

Coefficients act **multiplicatively** on the count. A slope $\beta$ means each
unit of $x$ multiplies the expected count by $e^{\beta}$. So $\beta = 0.69$
roughly **doubles** the count per unit ($e^{0.69}\approx 2$).

## Your task

Fit the Poisson model to `fish-counts.csv` and check your posterior means for
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ (both on the log scale).
